meeting_brief.previous_meeting: Quarterly Wellbeing and Career Progression Check-in
meeting_brief.meeting_objective: Review wellbeing, clarify promotion path, and agree on immediate retention actions.
key_takeaways: Alex reported sustained workload pressure across multiple sprints and described the workload as unsustainable.
Alex said he was passed over for promotion without a clear explanation and feels undervalued.
Alex highlighted ongoing manager conflict and uncertainty about his long-term career path on the team.
Alex requested concrete timelines, role clarity, and short-term workload support to prevent burnout.

action_items.task: Coordinate with Engineering Director and manager to provide documented promotion criteria and timeline within 10 business days.
Share a written list of workload blockers and role expectations before the follow-up meeting.
Set up a three-way follow-up with Alex and his manager to address working relationship concerns and role scope.

decisions_made: HR will run a structured intervention instead of an informal check-in due to high burnout risk.
A written promotion rubric and milestone plan will be provided to Alex.
A three-way alignment meeting (Alex, manager, HR) will be scheduled to resolve priority conflicts.

commitment_tracker.commitment: Send written meeting summary with owner-wise actions
Share promotion criteria and timeline document
Share blocker list and examples of conflicting priorities

unresolved_items: Promotion timeline is still not finalized.
Workload distribution and on-call ownership are not yet rebalanced.
Manager-employee communication norms are not formally agreed.
meeting_brief.next_steps: Hold a follow-up check-in in two weeks to review workload reduction, promotion clarity, and manager alignment.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Hi Alex, thanks for joining. Today is your quarterly wellbeing and growth check-in. How are you doing overall?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Honestly, not great. I have been running on low energy for a while and it feels like every sprint has been fire-fighting mode.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Thanks for being direct. Can you walk me through what has felt the most difficult over the last month?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: For at least five to six weeks, the workload has been too high. I am in late-night incident calls, then expected to deliver feature work the next morning. It has started affecting my work life balance.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That sounds unsustainable. Have you raised this with your manager, and did anything change?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: I did, multiple times. We had conversations, but there is still manager conflict around priorities. I am asked to own everything, yet I do not get support when timelines slip.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Understood. I want to ask about growth too. Last cycle you were aiming for Senior Engineer. How are you feeling about that now?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: I feel discouraged. I believe I was passed over for promotion without explanation. The feedback was vague, and I still do not know what concrete criteria I missed.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That is important feedback. Did you receive any written rubric or measurable expectations from leadership?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: No, and that is a big part of the frustration. I have led incidents, mentored juniors, and delivered migration work, but I still feel undervalued compared with peers who had clearer sponsorship.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: I hear you. Let us talk through day-to-day impact. Have these issues changed your engagement or communication patterns?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Yes. I have become less active in Slack and avoid optional channels because I am trying to keep my head above water. I am mostly responding only when tagged.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That aligns with what I noticed in communication trends. Are you considering any major changes if this does not improve?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: I am not making a decision today, but I cannot continue like this for another quarter. If there is no clarity and support soon, I will have to reassess my options.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Thank you for being transparent. When you say clarity, what are the top three things you need immediately?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: First, written promotion criteria. Second, a realistic workload split so I am not on incident and feature deadlines every week. Third, better manager alignment on priorities.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That is specific and helpful. On the manager alignment point, can you share one recent example where expectations were unclear?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Last sprint I was asked to lead release stabilization and also deliver two major backlog items. When I escalated risk, I was told to just make it work, then later asked why delivery slipped.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That sounds like conflicting direction. Did you document the escalation at the time?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Yes, in sprint comments and in Slack. But follow-through has been inconsistent, which is why I feel my concerns are not landing.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Understood. Let us also check wellbeing. How has this affected your health and recovery outside work?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Sleep has been irregular during release weeks, and I have used sick days mainly from stress and fatigue. I am trying to recover, but it keeps repeating.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: I appreciate your honesty. I want to make sure we address this quickly. Are you open to a structured recovery and workload plan starting this sprint?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Yes, I am open to it. I just need confidence that it is not temporary and that expectations will be realistic going forward.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: That is fair. I can set up a three-way meeting with your manager and include the engineering director if needed. Would that help?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Yes, that would help. I would like commitments in writing so there is a clear timeline and accountability.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Agreed. On career path, do you still want to pursue Senior Engineer here if we can restore clarity and support?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: I do, but right now my career path feels unclear. I need a concrete plan with milestones, not broad feedback.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: I can commit to that process. We will define milestones for technical scope, cross-team influence, and mentorship outcomes.

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: That would make a big difference. I want to keep contributing here, but I need to see concrete progress soon.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Let me summarize what I heard: sustained high workload, manager conflict around priorities, promotion clarity gap, and wellbeing impact. Did I capture that correctly?

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Yes, that is accurate.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Action plan: I will send written notes today, schedule the three-way discussion this week, and provide a promotion criteria document by next Friday.

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Thank you. Having timelines helps. I will also share my blocker list and examples so the conversation stays objective.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Jenkins
transcript.text: Great. We will do a follow-up in two weeks to review whether the workload and communication changes are actually working.

transcript.speaker: Alex Chen
transcript.text: Sounds good. I appreciate the support and I hope we can reset this in a sustainable way.
